DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jan-22-2020 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Jessica Beeler, Esq. of LEWIS BRISBOIS BISGAARD & SMITH LLP, T:415.438.5421, for defendant/moving party;
Ujvala Singh, Esq. of GORDON-CREED KELLEY, HOLL & SUGERMAN LLP, T:415.421.3100, for plaintiff/opposing party.

RULING: Defendant's counsel argues the tentative ruling. After arguments, the Court adopted its tentative ruling on DEFENDANTS BENJAMIN KAPLAN ESQ., DOUGLAS MACLELLAN's DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT, as follows:

Defendants' demurrer to the second cause of action of the complaint for breach of fiduciary duty is overruled. The court liberally construes the complaint per Code of Civil Procedure, 452. The scope of an attorney's fiduciary duty may be determined based on the Rules of Professional Conduct together with general principles relating to other fiduciary relationships. (See Stanley v. Richmond (1995) 35 Cal.App.4th 1070, 1086.) Plaintiff's allegations that defendants actively concealed errors and conflicts of interest, failed to keep plaintiff apprised of significant developments in the case, failed to communicate with plaintiff, charged an illegal fee, and breached the California Rules of Professional Conduct support the claim. Whether defendants actually breached their fiduciary duties is a factual issue. (Stanley, 35 Cal.App.4th at 1087.)

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Plaintiffs Complaint
Off Calendar Jan-22-2020 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Jessica Beeler, Esq. of LEWIS BRISBOIS BISGAARD & SMITH LLP, T:415.438.5421, for defendant/moving party;
Ujvala Singh, Esq. of GORDON-CREED KELLEY, HOLL & SUGERMAN LLP, T:415.421.3100, for plaintiff/opposing party.

RULING: Defendant's counsel argues the tentative ruling. After arguments, the Court adopted its tentative ruling on DEFENDANTS BENJAMIN KAPLAN ESQ., DOUGLAS MACLELLAN's Motion To Strike Plaintiff's Complaint, as follows:

Defendants' motion to strike is denied.

The motion is denied as to the claim for general damages. A party can recover emotional distress damages based on a breach of fiduciary duty. (See Stanley, 35 Cal.App.4th at 1097; Branch v. Homefed Bank (1992) 6 Cal.App.4th 793, 800 ["Recovery [of emotional distress damages] has also been allowed when the negligence arises in a situation involving breach of fiduciary or quasi-fiduciary duties?"]; see also, e.g., Knutson v. Foster (2018) 25 Cal.App.5th 1075, 1095-1099 [plaintiff may recover emotional distress damages against former attorney for breaches of fiduciary duty, including duty to keep client informed and failing to provide her with all the information she needed to make an informed decision about entering into settlement agreement, without need to present expert testimony].)

The court denies the motion to strike as to the claim for prejudgment interest. The court agrees that Civil Code, 3287 does not apply to this action because plaintiff's damages are unliquidated. Plaintiff may be able to recover prejudgment interest under Civil Code, 3288, however. That provision provides that "[i]n an action for the breach of an obligation not arising from contract, and in every case of oppression, fraud, or malice, interest may be given, in the discretion of the jury." It is well settled that the trier of fact has the discretion to award interest under Civil Code, 3288 for breach of a fiduciary relationship. (See Baker v. Pratt (1986) 176 Cal.App.3d 370, 383; Michelson v. Hamada (1994) 29 Cal.App.4th 1566, 1586 [awarding prejudgment interest per Civil Code, 3288 where plaintiff prevailed on both his breach of contract and breach of fiduciary duty causes of action.])

The court denies defendants' motion to strike the claim for attorney's fees. The complaint expressly seeks fees under the tort of another doctrine. (Complaint, 10:3.) "A person who through the tort of another has been required to act in the protection of his interests by bringing or defending an action against a third person is entitled to recover compensation for the reasonably necessary loss of time, attorney's fees, and other expenditures thereby suffered or incurred." (Prentice v. North Am. Title Guaranty Corp. (1963) 59 Cal.2d 618, 620.) In this case, plaintiff allegedly needed to retain another law firm to vindicate his rights under the Ellis Act and remedy plaintiff's alleged negligence. "Under California law, it is a well-established principle that attorney fees incurred through instituting or defending an action as a result of the tort of another are recoverable damages. [Citation.] We see no reason not to apply this principle to legal malpractice actions." (Sindell v. Gibson, Dunn & Crutcher (1997) 54 Cal.App.4th 1457, 1470.) Because plaintiff is relying on the tort of another doctrine, it is obvious that he is seeking the fees as damages and is not seeking fees to prosecute the instant action. (See Sooy v. Peter (1990) 220 Cal.App.3d 1305, 1310 [explaining that tort of another fees are damages].)

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com
